title: Sources, console and debugger

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for Sources, console and debugger; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: MDN JavaScript Guide; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Sources, console and debugger
sources.claim: Browser DevTools expose sources, breakpoints, network, console, DOM, storage, and performance evidence for one runtime state.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Browser DevTools expose sources, breakpoints, network, console, DOM, storage, and performance evidence for one runtime state.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A breakpoint confirms an event payload before code is changed and a regression test preserves the finding. Your goal is to use developer tools to locate and explain a state-transition defect. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: pf computing execution; html document skeleton; Scripts and console; pf tools debug test. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Browser DevTools expose sources, breakpoints, network, console, DOM, storage, and performance evidence for one runtime state. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Console evaluation can mutate state, source maps can mislead, and local overrides are not persisted automatically. This draft therefore rejects: The console is only for printing and cannot alter application behavior.

includes: Set breakpoints, inspect scope and distinguish logged values from live object views.
Use developer tools to locate and explain a state-transition defect.
excludes: Console evaluation can mutate state, source maps can mislead, and local overrides are not persisted automatically.

outcomes: Use developer tools to locate and explain a state-transition defect.

examples.title: Sources, console and debugger: concrete case
examples.situation: A breakpoint confirms an event payload before code is changed and a regression test preserves the finding.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A breakpoint confirms an event payload before code is changed and a regression test preserves the finding.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Treat DevTools as an observation environment that can also mutate state; verify changes in source and tests.
examples.result: The result follows because Treat DevTools as an observation environment that can also mutate state; verify changes in source and tests.

examples.title: Sources, console and debugger: boundary case
examples.situation: Console evaluation can mutate state, source maps can mislead, and local overrides are not persisted automatically.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Console evaluation can mutate state, source maps can mislead, and local overrides are not persisted automatically.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: The console is only for printing and cannot alter application behavior.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Treat DevTools as an observation environment that can also mutate state; verify changes in source and tests.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: The console is only for printing and cannot alter application behavior.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications artifact to explain why this belief fails: The console is only for printing and cannot alter application behavior.

analogy.example: A coordinated event desk can model Sources, console and debugger, with values, tasks, callbacks, and external messages changing observable state.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Sources, console and debugger before using exact platform terms
limitations: Human coordination does not reproduce coercion, lexical scope, microtasks, prototypes, or browser security boundaries.

recap.summary: You should now be able to use developer tools to locate and explain a state-transition defect. Keep this boundary in view: Console evaluation can mutate state, source maps can mislead, and local overrides are not persisted automatically.
